policy_statement: Develop an alternative energy master plan focused on low-carbon solutions such as district energy, solar, and geothermal energy in the public right-of-way (by 2015).
mechanism_description: Developing a low-carbon alternative energy master plan supports adoption of district energy, solar, and geothermal energy, which displaces higher-carbon energy supply and reduces emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Energy planning + infrastructure deployment → fossil energy displacement → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: The city develops and uses a master plan to guide investment, siting, and permitting for alternative energy systems (district energy networks, solar PV/thermal, geothermal), including use of public right-of-way.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Increased availability and adoption of low/zero-carbon electricity and thermal energy reduces demand for combustion of fossil fuels (on-site boilers/furnaces) and/or high-carbon grid electricity by substituting cleaner supply.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct (Scope 1) and indirect (Scope 2) CO₂e emissions from energy production and use over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing GHG emissions by shifting energy supply toward low/zero-carbon sources that displace fossil generation/combustion.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: planning to enable low/zero-carbon energy supply → displacement of fossil energy → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the plan can improve energy system efficiency or resilience, its core stated purpose is decarbonizing energy supply (district energy/solar/geothermal), which is primarily a mitigation pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a plan were primarily for reliability, redundancy, or emergency energy during extreme events, it could be adaptation; if focused mainly on reducing energy use (efficiency) rather than changing supply, it could be resource efficiency. Those are not the dominant intent/effect described here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any “low-carbon/alternative energy master plan” whose purpose is to expand low/zero-carbon supply options functions chiefly by displacing higher-carbon supply and cutting emissions.  
5) Note on subtypes: Any secondary subtype (e.g., renewable electricity vs low-carbon thermal, municipal operations vs community-wide) depends on policy-specific implementation details and is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Master planning is an enabling action; realized mitigation depends on subsequent implementation. Some district energy systems could be fossil-fueled CHP if not explicitly low-carbon—classification assumes the mechanism’s stated low-carbon intent (solar/geothermal, low-carbon district energy).
key_indicators: “Develop an alternative energy master plan”; “focuses on low-carbon energy solutions”; “district energy, solar energy, and geothermal”; “public right-of-way”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements; potential energy cost stability/resilience benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Plan development alone does not deploy infrastructure; impacts depend on subsequent implementation actions/investments guided by the master plan.
